article_metadata.title: Denatured Whey Protein Powder as a New Matrix Excipient: Design and Evaluation of Mucoadhesive Tablets for Sustained Drug Release Applications

sections_likely_locations.materials_origin: Materials; Preparation of DWP Powders; Characterization of DWP Powders
sections_likely_locations.experimental_design_for_optimization: Preparation of Matrix Tablets; Characterization of DWP Matrix Tablets; Evaluation of Drug Release; Analysis of Drug Release Mechanisms; Artificial Digestion of Drug Loaded-Tablets
sections_likely_locations.ingredients_identification_absorbance: Materials; Preparation of DWP Powders; Characterization of DWP Powders (solubility by UV at 280 nm; theophylline at 273 nm)
sections_likely_locations.formulation_details: Preparation of Matrix Tablets; Table I
sections_likely_locations.precompression_parameters: Physical Characteristics and Compressibility of Powders; Powders Flow Properties; Table I
sections_likely_locations.independent_variables_and_levels: Preparation of Matrix Tablets; Table I
sections_likely_locations.tablet_fabrication: Preparation of Matrix Tablets; Pharmacotechnical Characterization
sections_likely_locations.process_parameters: Preparation of DWP Powders; Preparation of Matrix Tablets; In Vitro Drug Dissolution Tests; Sequential Drug Release Kinetics
sections_likely_locations.release_properties: Evaluation of Drug Release (Dissolution Profiles in Enzyme Free Buffers; Influence of pH Shift; Influence of Gastric and Intestinal Enzymes); Analysis of Drug Release Mechanisms
sections_likely_locations.tablet_properties: Pharmacotechnical Characterization; Swelling and Erosion Studies; Mucoadhesive Behavior; Evaluation of Drug Release
sections_likely_locations.experimental_data: Results and Discussion throughout (Physical Characteristics, Swelling/Erosion, Mucoadhesion, Dissolution, Modeling, Artificial Digestive System)

whey_protein_isolate.description: Whey protein isolate (WPI 89.5%) used to prepare native and denatured whey protein solutions

denatured_whey_protein_powder_preparatio.starting_material: WPI powder rehydrated in deionized water to 10% w/w
denatured_whey_protein_powder_preparatio.rehydration_conditions: Gentle magnetic stirring 1 h at room temperature, then 2 h rest

experimental_design_for_optimization.goal: Evaluate feasibility of direct compression tablets using DWP powder as matrix/mucoadhesive excipient and study their controlled release behavior with theophylline.
experimental_design_for_optimization.design_type: Formulation screening with increasing DWP content in directly compressed matrix tablets; multiple in vitro dissolution conditions and mathematical modeling.

formulations.code_scheme: 017_F0–017_F5 for drug-loaded tablets; F0a–F4a for placebo (no drug) tablets.
formulations.independent_variable: DWP percentage in the tablet blend (0, 10, 15, 20, 30, 40% nominal for 017_F0–017_F5).
responses: Powder flow and compressibility (bulk/tapped density, CI, HR)
Tablet hardness, friability, weight, disintegration time
Swelling and erosion (water uptake, volume change, matrix weight loss)
Theophylline dissolution in various pH media with/without enzymes
Release kinetics parameters (Higuchi, Korsmeyer-Peppas, first order, Weibull)
Mucoadhesion on excised pig intestinal mucosa
Drug release/absorption in the artificial digestive system TIM-1 for 017_F4

proteins.method: Excess NWP or DWP dispersed in water or buffers (pH 1.2, 4.5, 6.8), 24 h equilibration, centrifugation, spectrophotometric assay.
proteins.analysis: Protein concentration in supernatant from calibration curves to calculate solubility and DWP release.

standard_dissolution.apparatus: UV spectrophotometry on dissolution samples from USP 2 paddle tests

sequential_media_with_protein.mobile_phase: 100% eluent A: 9% acetonitrile in 0.01 M acetate buffer pH 4

formulation_details_each_batch.note: Quantitative tablet composition is given in Table I; the article uses 017_F0–017_F5, not 017_F0–017_F6, and 017_F5 (40% DWP) was not successfully tableted for all tests.

017_F1.mcc_qs: Balance after adding the above components

017_F4.comment: Selected as optimized; used in mucoadhesion tests and TIM-1.

placebo_batches.F0a_to_F4a: Same composition as 017_F0–017_F4 but theophylline replaced by equivalent mass of MCC; used for whey protein release and erosion tests.

precompression_parameters_each_batch.note: Reported in Table I as blend properties, not strictly ‘pre-compression’ tablet data.

tablet_mechanical_properties.note: Hardness and friability reported for 017_F0–017_F4 tablets; values are means with SD where provided.

controlled_variables: Compression tooling: 12 mm flat cylindrical punches
Target hardness: ≥ 60 N; typically around 70–85 N

blend_preparation: Mix theophylline with DWP and MCC for 20 min.
Add magnesium stearate (1% w/w) and Aerosil (0.5% w/w) and mix additional 10 min.

compression.equipment: Alternative tableting press machine (Korsch, Berlin, Germany)
compression.mode: Direct compression of final powder blend

equipment_pharmacotechnical_tests.swelling_erosion: USP 2 dissolution vessels with paddle, 1000 mL, 37 °C, 60 rpm

batch_specific_tablet_properties.note: Density and consolidation index not explicitly given; CI refers to powder compressibility index, not tablet consolidation index.

compression_process.device: Korsch single-station/alternative tableting press (type not further specified)
compression_process.pressure: Not explicitly reported; hardness controlled to ≥ 60 N.

media: Simulated gastric fluid (SGF) with and without pepsin
Simulated intestinal fluid (SIF) with and without pancreatin
dissolution_test_conditions.sampling: 1 mL, replaced by fresh medium; up to 12 h.

sequential_dissolution_protocol.analysis: Theophylline quantified by RP-HPLC at 273 nm.

artificial_digestive_system_TIM1.pH_control: Computer-controlled HCl (0.3 M) and NaHCO3 (0.5 M) additions
artificial_digestive_system_TIM1.dose_introduced: One 017_F4 tablet with 200 g of water for fasted conditions.

general.media: USP buffers pH 1.2, 4.5, 6.8; SGF/SIF with and without enzymes; TIM-1.
general.overall_mechanism_summary: Primarily diffusion-controlled in enzyme-free media; shifts toward erosion-accelerated release in presence of proteolytic enzymes.

release_profile.enzyme_free: Immediate-release: >80% drug in <45 min in all pH; complete release by 2–3 h.
release_profile.sequential: Similar to single pH tests; no pH-shift effects.

release_kinetics.dominant_model: First-order/Higuchi typical of IR matrix with soluble drug
release_kinetics.mechanism: Dissolution of theophylline from non-gelling MCC matrix.

release_profile.enzyme_free: Slower than 017_F0 but still relatively fast; incomplete release at pH 4.5 (~70% at 6 h, no disintegration up to 6 h); ~complete in 24 h at pH 1.2 and 6.8.
release_profile.sequential_without_enzymes: Similar shape to single pH but T50 and T80 extended; compacted structure at pH 4.5 impacts subsequent release.
release_profile.with_enzymes: Marked acceleration; release fits Korsmeyer-Peppas with higher kKP, n ≈ 0.66; erosion more prominent.

release_profile.enzyme_free: Clear sustained release; T50 ~ 6 h, T80 ~ 16 h; complete release by 24 h; similar profiles at pH 1.2 and 6.8, slightly slower at pH 4.5.
release_profile.sequential_without_enzymes: Further retardation vs individual pH; T50 extended to ~8 h.
release_profile.with_enzymes: Faster and more complete release; erosion-driven plus diffusion.

release_profile.enzyme_free: Similar to 017_F2 and 017_F4; controlled release with T50 ~ 6 h, T80 ~ 16 h, complete by 24 h across pH.
release_profile.sequential_without_enzymes: T50 ~ 8 h; similar non-Fickian diffusion-controlled behavior.
release_profile.with_enzymes: Faster release; erosion pronounced; complete release by ~8 h.

release_profile.enzyme_free: Robust sustained release; T50 ~ 6 h, T80 ~ 16 h, complete release in 24 h; similar profiles at pH 1.2 and 6.8, slightly slower at pH 4.5 due to proximity to protein pI.
release_profile.sequential_without_enzymes: T50 extended to ~8 h due to shrinkage/compaction at pH 4.5 influencing subsequent pH 6.8 stage.
release_profile.with_enzymes: The most resistant among DWP formulations; still accelerated release vs enzyme-free but with slower erosion; complete release by ~8 h.

table_IIIb.comment: Erosion and diffusion both contribute; kKP increased vs enzyme-free.

free_theophylline.profile: Fast absorption typical of BCS I.

drug_loaded_dwp_powder.profile: Spray-dried powder does not significantly slow release.

tablet_F4.profile: Clear sustained absorption; confirms matrix functionality under dynamic GIT conditions.

017_F5.comment: Not taken forward into tablet dissolution studies due to poor flow and inability to compress robust tablets.

F1_10_percent.behavior: High water uptake leading to disintegration and erosion; tablets disintegrated after 4 h at pH 1.2 and 6.8 and after 6 h at pH 4.5.
F1_10_percent.DWP_release: Pronounced; >65% released by 6 h at pH 1.2 and 6.8; ~50% at pH 4.5.

F2_to_F4_15_to_30_percent.behavior: Rapid water uptake (~150% within 30 min) with strong swelling; matrix weight loss limited (DWP release <10% by 12 h); gel layer forms; inner core remains intact up to 4 h then fully gelled.
F2_to_F4_15_to_30_percent.threshold_effect: Above 20% DWP, water uptake becomes limited and swelling somewhat reduced; 017_F4 shows lower water uptake than 017_F2–F3.

mucoadhesion.test: Wash-off test on porcine intestinal mucosa at 37 °C in pH 6.8 buffer with up-and-down motion (USP disintegration apparatus).

017_F2.adhesion_result: ≈33% of tablets still adhered at 15 min; lower mucoadhesion.

017_F4.adhesion_result: 100% of tablets adhered up to 60 min; strong mucoadhesive properties.

mucoadhesion.mechanism: Mucoadhesion attributed to DWP denaturation exposing free thiol and hydrophilic groups, enabling hydrogen bonding and disulfide bonds with mucin; higher DWP content increases available functional groups.

tablet_properties_and_swelling.stability: No long-term stability study of tablets reported; only in vitro performance and TIM-1 digestion.

dwp_release_from_placebo_tablets.result: DWP release almost zero up to 4 h for ≥15% DWP; ~7% after 12 h; no pH influence.

in_vitro.kinetic_modeling: Weibull, Higuchi, Korsmeyer-Peppas, first-order fits; see 017_F1–017_F4 entries and Table III for representative r² and parameters.

in_vivo_or_ex_vivo.mucoadhesion_ex_vivo: Adhesion percentages for 017_F2 vs 017_F4 on pig intestine; 017_F4 clearly superior.
in_vivo_or_ex_vivo.tim1_ads: Level-A style correlation context cited; 017_F4 tablets show extended absorption vs free drug.
